source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.2 (split from vector_basics bundle — STATE_1 and STATE_5 content)

real_world_anchor.primary: A car moving east at 20 m/s. Double the speed — still east, but 40 m/s. Reverse gear at the same speed — west, 20 m/s. The number you multiply by decides both size and direction.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A rickshaw pulled by a rope. Pull with twice the force, same rope direction — vector doubles. Push instead of pull with the same strength — direction flips.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Zooming a photo on your phone. Scale factor 2 makes the arrow on screen twice as long; scale factor −1 would flip it head-to-tail.

formulas.direction_rule: Same as A if m > 0;  opposite if m < 0;  zero vector if m = 0

constraints: Magnitude is always non-negative: |mA| ≥ 0, regardless of sign of m
The sign of m decides direction — not the sign of magnitude
m = 0 collapses any vector to the zero vector
Distributive and associative laws hold exactly — scalars obey ordinary algebra

STATE_1.title: Double a Velocity — Same Road, Twice the Speed

scene_composition.text:
Multiply A by 2.
Length doubles.
Direction unchanged.

tts_sentences.text_en: A car moves east at four metres per second. That is the vector A.
Double the speed — same road — and the vector becomes two times A, which is eight metres per second east.
The arrow is twice as long. The direction has not changed at all.
This is scalar multiplication with a positive number. Scale the length, keep the direction.

STATE_2.title: Negative Scalar — Direction Flips, Magnitude Scales

scene_composition.text:
Multiply A by −2.
|m| = 2  →  length doubles.
Sign is negative  →  direction flips.

|−2A| = 2 |A|.  NOT −2 |A|.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now multiply A by a negative number — say minus two.
The magnitude is the absolute value of the scalar, times A. So the arrow becomes twice as long.
The negative sign does a different job — it flips the direction one hundred eighty degrees. What was east now points west.
Crucial — magnitude is always positive. Never say the magnitude of minus two A is minus two A. Magnitude is a length, length cannot be negative.

scene_composition.text:
Magnitude rule:
|mA| = |m| × |A|

Direction rule:
m > 0  →  same as A
m < 0  →  opposite to A
m = 0  →  zero vector (no direction)

Examples:
|3A|   = 3 |A|,   direction  same as A
|−5A|  = 5 |A|,   direction  opposite to A
|0 × A| = 0,      zero vector
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: One magnitude rule, one direction rule. That is all you need for scalar multiplication.
Magnitude rule — the magnitude of m times A equals the absolute value of m, multiplied by the magnitude of A.
Direction rule — positive m preserves direction, negative m flips it, zero m collapses to the zero vector.
Memorise these two. Every scalar multiplication problem reduces to applying them.

STATE_4.title: The Algebra Works — (m + n)A = mA + nA

scene_composition.text:
Take m = 2, n = 3, A = 5 east.

(m + n) A = (2 + 3) × 5 east = 5A = 25 east

mA + nA  = 2A + 3A   = 10 + 15 = 25 east

They match.  Distributivity holds exactly.

tts_sentences.text_en: Scalars distribute over vector multiplication — exactly like ordinary numbers.
Take m equals two, n equals three, A equals five east. The sum on the left gives five A, which is twenty five east.
Compute the right side separately. Two A is ten east. Three A is fifteen east. Add them head to tail — twenty five east.
Both sides match. This is why scalar algebra works on vectors — scalars themselves obey ordinary arithmetic.
The associative rule, m times n times A equals mn times A, follows the same pattern. All of this is safe to use in calculations.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Multiplying by a negative scalar gives the vector a negative magnitude
trigger_phrases: what is magnitude of minus 2 A
can magnitude be negative when scalar is negative

STATE_1.title: Show the Wrong Belief — A Negative Length

scene_composition.text:
Student writes:
|−2A| = −2 |A|
|−2 × 5| = −10

But what is an arrow of length −10?
Which direction? Which size?
Show the Wrong Belief — A Negative Length
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: A common error — students write the magnitude of minus two A as minus two times magnitude of A.
If that were true, multiplying a five-unit vector by minus two would give an arrow of length minus ten.
But what is an arrow of length minus ten? Length is a distance. Distance cannot be negative.

STATE_2.title: The Two Jobs of the Minus Sign

scene_composition.text:
The minus sign does TWO things —
but one at a time.

On a scalar quantity:
   −5 is a number below zero.

On a vector direction:
   −A flips the arrow 180°.

It never makes a LENGTH negative.
The Two Jobs of the Minus Sign
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: The minus sign has two jobs in mathematics.
On a scalar, minus means less than zero — a temperature of minus five, a score of minus three.
On a vector direction, minus means flip one hundred eighty degrees.
It never takes a length and makes it negative. Length is always non-negative.

STATE_3.title: The Correct Formula — Step by Step

scene_composition.text:
|mA|  =  |m × A|        (by definition)
      =  |m| × |A|     (abs-value splits)
      =  |m| × |A|      (|A| already ≥ 0)

Example  A = 5 east, m = −2:
   |−2A|  =  |−2| × |A|  =  2 × 5  =  10

Magnitude is 10. Direction is WEST (because m < 0).
The Correct Formula — Step by Step
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the correct derivation. Magnitude of m A equals absolute value of m, times absolute value of A.
The absolute value of the scalar is always positive or zero. The magnitude of A is always positive or zero.
Their product cannot be negative. Ever.
For minus two A with A equals five east, magnitude is ten. Direction is west — because the sign of m is negative.

regeneration_variants.label: Different numbers, same rule
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = 8 m/s north, find 3A and −(1/2)A. Magnitudes: 24 and 4. Directions: north and south.

regeneration_variants.label: Force scaling on a spring
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Apply a force F to a spring. Double it (2F) — same direction, spring stretches twice as far. Reverse it (−F) — spring compresses by the original stretch.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Magnitude scales with |m|; direction flips with sign of m
